## [Link to Training Rerankers](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#training-rerankers) Training RerankersLink Copied

Training a cross-encoder reranker requires supervised data consisting of query-document pairs labeled with relevance judgments. Unlike [bi-encoders](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders), which we trained using contrastive objectives in [Contrastive Learning for Retrieval](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/contrastive-learning-retrieval.qmd), cross-encoders can leverage standard classification or ranking losses because they process pairs jointly. This joint processing means that the model can learn directly from explicit relevance signals rather than needing to construct contrastive pairs that approximate the ranking task. The availability of labeled relevance judgments, typically obtained through human annotators or implicit feedback signals like clicks, allows cross-encoders to optimize directly for the probability of relevance or relative ranking preferences.

### [Link to Binary Classification](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#binary-classification) Binary ClassificationLink Copied

The simplest approach treats relevance as a [binary classification](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/logistic-regression-complete-guide-mathematical-foundations-python-implementation) problem. Given a dataset {(qi,di,yi)}i=1N\\{(q\_i, d\_i, y\_i)\\}\_{i=1}^N{(qi​,di​,yi​)}i=1N​ where yi∈{0,1}y\_i \\in \\{0, 1\\}yi​∈{0,1} indicates relevance, you minimize [cross-entropy loss](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/cross-entropy-loss-language-models-information-theory):

L=−1N∑i=1N\[yilog⁡σ(s(qi,di))+(1−yi)log⁡(1−σ(s(qi,di)))\]\\mathcal{L} = -\\frac{1}{N} \\sum\_{i=1}^N \\left\[ y\_i \\log \\sigma(s(q\_i, d\_i)) + (1 - y\_i) \\log(1 - \\sigma(s(q\_i, d\_i))) \\right\]L=−N1​i=1∑N​\[yi​logσ(s(qi​,di​))+(1−yi​)log(1−σ(s(qi​,di​)))\]

where:

- NNN: number of training examples
- qiq\_iqi​: the iii-th query in the training set
- did\_idi​: the iii-th document in the training set
- yi∈{0,1}y\_i \\in \\{0, 1\\}yi​∈{0,1}: binary relevance label (1 if relevant, 0 if irrelevant)
- s(qi,di)s(q\_i, d\_i)s(qi​,di​): predicted relevance score for the iii-th pair
- σ(⋅)\\sigma(\\cdot)σ(⋅): sigmoid function converting scores to probabilities
- L\\mathcal{L}L: the cross-entropy loss measuring prediction error

This works well when you have explicit relevance labels, such as click-through data or human judgments on a binary scale (relevant/irrelevant). However, collecting reliable binary labels presents significant challenges. Human annotators often disagree on relevance boundaries, and click-through data can be noisy due to position bias (users are more likely to click top results regardless of relevance) and presentation bias (attractive titles may generate clicks even for irrelevant documents). Active learning strategies can help prioritize which documents to label, focusing annotation effort on the boundary cases where the model is most uncertain. Additionally, class imbalance is common in retrieval datasets: for most queries, the vast majority of documents are irrelevant, so you may need to use weighted loss functions or careful sampling strategies to prevent the model from learning to always predict "irrelevant."

### [Link to Pairwise Ranking Loss](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#pairwise-ranking-loss) Pairwise Ranking LossLink Copied

Often, you have relative judgments: for queryqqq, documentd+d^+d+ is more relevant than d−d^-d−. The margin ranking loss encourages the model to score the positive higher than the negative by at least a marginmmm:

L=∑(q,d+,d−)max⁡(0,m−s(q,d+)+s(q,d−))\\mathcal{L} = \\sum\_{(q, d^+, d^-)} \\max(0, m - s(q, d^+) + s(q, d^-))L=(q,d+,d−)∑​max(0,m−s(q,d+)+s(q,d−))

where:

- qqq: the query being processed
- d+d^+d+: a document labeled as relevant (positive) to queryqqq
- d−d^-d−: a document labeled as irrelevant (negative) to queryqqq
- s(q,d)s(q, d)s(q,d): relevance score assigned to query-document pair (q,d)(q, d)(q,d)
- mmm: margin hyperparameter (typically set to 1.0) specifying the minimum desired score difference
- L\\mathcal{L}L: the margin ranking loss penalizing violations of the ranking constraint

This is analogous to the hinge loss used in support vector machines. If s(q,d+)≥s(q,d−)+ms(q, d^+) \\geq s(q, d^-) + ms(q,d+)≥s(q,d−)+m, the loss is zero; otherwise, it increases linearly with the violation. The marginmmm is a hyperparameter, typically set to 1.0. The intuition behind the margin is that you want the model to not only rank the positive above the negative but to do so with confidence. A small score difference might indicate uncertainty, while a large margin suggests the model has learned a robust distinction between the two documents.

Building on our understanding of the [Bradley-Terry model](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/preference-evaluation-pairwise-comparisons-elo-llm) from [Bradley-Terry Model](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/bradley-terry-model.qmd), we can also interpret pairwise preferences probabilistically. The probability that d+d^+d+ is preferred over d−d^-d− is:

where:

- d+≻d−d^+ \\succ d^-d+≻d−: event indicating documentd+d^+d+ is preferred over documentd−d^-d−
- s(q,d+)s(q, d^+)s(q,d+): relevance score for the positive document
- s(q,d−)s(q, d^-)s(q,d−): relevance score for the negative document
- σ(⋅)\\sigma(\\cdot)σ(⋅): [sigmoid function](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/logistic-regression-complete-guide-mathematical-foundations-python-implementation) converting score differences to probabilities
- P(d+≻d−∣q)P(d^+ \\succ d^- \| q)P(d+≻d−∣q): probability that d+d^+d+ is more relevant than d−d^-d− given queryqqq

Maximizing the log-likelihood of observed preferences yields the logistic loss:

L=−∑log⁡σ(s(q,d+)−s(q,d−))\\mathcal{L} = -\\sum \\log \\sigma(s(q, d^+) - s(q, d^-))L=−∑logσ(s(q,d+)−s(q,d−))

where:

- s(q,d+)s(q, d^+)s(q,d+): relevance score for the positive document
- s(q,d−)s(q, d^-)s(q,d−): relevance score for the negative document
- σ(⋅)\\sigma(\\cdot)σ(⋅): sigmoid function
- L\\mathcal{L}L: negative log-likelihood loss (logistic loss)

This formulation, used in RankNet and similar neural ranking models, naturally handles noisy preferences and provides well-calibrated probability estimates. Unlike the margin ranking loss, which treats preference violations with uniform penalty regardless of magnitude (beyond the margin), the logistic loss penalizes violations based on their probability, with larger mistakes receiving exponentially higher penalties. This probabilistic interpretation also allows you to combine evidence from multiple annotators or to model uncertainty in the preference labels themselves.

### [Link to Listwise Approaches](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#listwise-approaches) Listwise ApproachesLink Copied

While pairwise losses compare documents two at a time, listwise approaches consider the entire ranked list for a query. These methods often optimize metrics closer to what you actually care about, such as Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG). The key insight behind listwise approaches is that ranking quality depends not just on individual document scores but on the relative ordering of all documents in the list. A pairwise approach might correctly order 99 pairs but still produce a suboptimal list if it makes a critical error at the top position, while a listwise approach can directly optimize for the importance of top-ranked positions.

One popular listwise loss is ListNet, which treats ranking as a probability distribution over permutations. For a list of documents D={d1,…,dk}D = \\{d\_1, \\dots, d\_k\\}D={d1​,…,dk​}, define the probability of documentdid\_idi​ being at the top using the [softmax](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/multinomial-logistic-regression-complete-guide-mathematical-foundations-python-implementation) over scores:

P(di)=exp⁡(s(q,di))∑j=1kexp⁡(s(q,dj))P(d\_i) = \\frac{\\exp(s(q, d\_i))}{\\sum\_{j=1}^k \\exp(s(q, d\_j))}P(di​)=∑j=1k​exp(s(q,dj​))exp(s(q,di​))​

where:

- did\_idi​: the iii-th document in the list of kkk candidates
- s(q,di)s(q, d\_i)s(q,di​): relevance score assigned to documentdid\_idi​ by the model
- kkk: number of documents in the ranked list
- exp⁡(⋅)\\exp(\\cdot)exp(⋅): exponential function ensuring positive values
- P(di)P(d\_i)P(di​): probability that documentdid\_idi​ should be ranked at the top position

Similarly, define target probabilities P∗(di)P^\*(d\_i)P∗(di​) based on relevance grades (e.g., exponential decay with position in the ideal ranking). The loss is the cross- [entropy](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/cart-decision-trees-classification-regression-mathematical-foundations-python-implementation) between these distributions:

L=−∑i=1kP∗(di)log⁡P(di)\\mathcal{L} = -\\sum\_{i=1}^k P^\*(d\_i) \\log P(d\_i)L=−i=1∑k​P∗(di​)logP(di​)

where:

- kkk: number of documents in the list
- P∗(di)P^\*(d\_i)P∗(di​): target probability for documentdid\_idi​ (derived from relevance grades)
- P(di)P(d\_i)P(di​): predicted probability from the softmax over scores
- L\\mathcal{L}L: [cross-entropy loss](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/cross-entropy-loss-language-models-information-theory) between target and predicted ranking distributions

Listwise losses can better model the dependencies between documents and directly optimize for ranking quality, though they are computationally more intensive due to the need to process entire lists. They also allow you to incorporate position-dependent importance naturally, since the loss is sensitive to which documents appear at the top of the list. However, computing these losses over large lists can be expensive, and the [softmax](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/multinomial-logistic-regression-complete-guide-mathematical-foundations-python-implementation) [normalization](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/normalization-feature-scaling-min-max-machine-learning-guide) can become numerically unstable with large score ranges. Variants like ListMLE simplify the computation by focusing on the probability of the observed permutation rather than the full distribution over all possible permutations.

### [Link to Hard Negative Mining](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#hard-negative-mining) Hard Negative MiningLink Copied

A critical aspect of training rerankers is [negative sampling](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/negative-sampling-word-embeddings). If you train only on easy negatives (randomly sampled documents), the model learns little beyond what the [bi-encoder](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) already knows. The cross-encoder must learn to distinguish between documents that appear relevant to a fast retriever but are actually unsuitable for answering the query. Without [hard negatives](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/contrastive-learning-retrieval-infonce-dpr), the reranker fails to develop the discriminative capacity needed to improve upon the initial retrieval.

Instead, you should mine hard negatives: documents that the bi-encoder retrieves but are actually irrelevant. These are documents that share semantic similarity with the query, perhaps containing overlapping vocabulary or related concepts, but fail to satisfy the specific information need. Hard negatives teach the model the subtle distinctions that separate plausible-looking documents from truly relevant ones.

The standard procedure is:

1. Retrieve top-kkk documents for each training query using your bi-encoder
2. Filter out truly relevant documents (based on your labeled data)
3. Use the remaining retrieved documents as negatives

This forces the cross-encoder to learn the subtle distinctions that separate plausible-looking but ultimately irrelevant documents from truly relevant ones. As training progresses, you should periodically refresh the hard negatives using the current best retrieval model, a technique known as iterative [hard negative mining](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/contrastive-learning-retrieval-infonce-dpr). This iterative process creates a curriculum where the model progressively learns to distinguish finer and finer distinctions. Early in training, the model benefits from easier negatives to learn basic semantic distinctions. As the model improves, harder negatives force it to focus on increasingly subtle cues. However, care must be taken not to select negatives that are too hard: if a negative document is actually relevant (a [false negative](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/type-i-type-ii-errors-false-positives-false-negatives-statistical-power)), training on it as a negative can degrade model performance. Quality control of negative mining is essential, often requiring human validation of edge cases or the use of multiple retrieval systems to identify likely true negatives.

## [Link to Latency Considerations and Optimization](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#latency-considerations-and-optimization) Latency Considerations and OptimizationLink Copied

The primary barrier to deploying cross-encoders is latency. If your retrieval system returns 100 candidates and your cross-encoder requires 50ms per document, reranking adds 5 seconds of latency, unacceptable for most interactive applications. In web search or conversational AI systems, users expect sub-second response times, often targeting latencies below 200 milliseconds. When the cross-encoder becomes the bottleneck, you must either reduce the number of documents processed, reduce the time per document, or apply the cross-encoder selectively.

### [Link to Selective Reranking](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#selective-reranking) Selective RerankingLink Copied

You need not rerank all retrieved documents. Confidence-based filtering uses the [bi-encoder](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) scores to identify high-confidence candidates. If the top bi-encoder result has a score significantly higher than the rest, you might return it directly without reranking, applying the cross-encoder only to ambiguous cases where scores are close. This approach recognizes that for many queries, the bi-encoder is sufficiently confident in its top prediction that the expensive reranking provides minimal benefit. By measuring the gap between the top score and the mean or second-highest score, you can estimate the uncertainty of the initial retrieval and allocate computational resources only where ambiguity exists. This strategy requires careful calibration to ensure that high-confidence predictions are indeed accurate; otherwise, you risk bypassing the reranker precisely when it would have caught a retrieval error.

### [Link to Document Truncation](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#document-truncation) Document TruncationLink Copied

Cross-encoder complexity grows quadratically with sequence length. For long documents, you typically truncate to the first Lmax⁡L\_{\\max}Lmax​ tokens (often 512 or 1024 tokens). However, relevant information might appear at the end of a document, a phenomenon known as the " [lost in the middle](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/context-length-challenges-transformers)" problem. Early portions of documents often contain boilerplate, introductions, or general context, while specific answers may appear later. Sliding window approaches split long documents into overlapping chunks, rerank each chunk independently, and aggregate scores:

s(q,d)=max⁡c∈chunks(d)s(q,c)s(q, d) = \\max\_{c \\in \\text{chunks}(d)} s(q, c)s(q,d)=c∈chunks(d)max​s(q,c)

where:

- ddd: the full document being scored
- ccc: a specific chunk (text segment) of the document
- chunks(d)\\text{chunks}(d)chunks(d): set of overlapping text segments extracted from documentddd
- s(q,c)s(q, c)s(q,c): relevance score between queryqqq and chunk ccc
- s(q,d)s(q, d)s(q,d): final document score, computed as the maximum score across all chunks

This ensures the cross-encoder sees all parts of the document, though it multiplies computational cost by the number of chunks. Alternative aggregation strategies include taking the average of the top few chunk scores or using a learned aggregation function. The choice of [chunk size](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/document-chunking-rag-strategies-retrieval) and overlap involves tradeoffs: smaller chunks reduce the quadratic cost per chunk but may lose context that spans chunk boundaries, while larger chunks preserve more context but increase computational cost.

### [Link to Late Interaction Models](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#late-interaction-models) Late Interaction ModelsLink Copied

Researchers have developed architectures that balance the efficiency of [bi-encoders](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) with the accuracy of cross-encoders. Late interaction models, exemplified by [ColBERT](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/multi-vector-retrievers-fine-grained-token-level-matching-for-neural-information-retrieval) (Contextualized Late Interaction over [BERT](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/bert-bidirectional-pretraining-revolutionizes-language-understanding)), encode query and document separately but allow token-level interactions at scoring time:

s(q,d)=∑i=1Lqmax⁡j=1LdqiTdjs(q, d) = \\sum\_{i=1}^{L\_q} \\max\_{j=1}^{L\_d} \\mathbf{q}\_i^T \\mathbf{d}\_js(q,d)=i=1∑Lq​​j=1maxLd​​qiT​dj​

where:

- LqL\_qLq​: number of tokens in the query
- LdL\_dLd​: number of tokens in the document
- qi∈Rd\\mathbf{q}\_i \\in \\mathbb{R}^dqi​∈Rd: contextualized embedding of the iii-th querytoken
- dj∈Rd\\mathbf{d}\_j \\in \\mathbb{R}^ddj​∈Rd: contextualized embedding of the jjj-th documenttoken
- qiTdj\\mathbf{q}\_i^T \\mathbf{d}\_jqiT​dj​: [dot product similarity](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/vector-similarity-search-metrics-ann-faiss) between querytokeniii and documenttokenjjj
- s(q,d)s(q, d)s(q,d): final relevance score (sum of maximum similarities for each querytoken)

The query and document are encoded independently (fast, precomputable), but the scoring involves a maximum similarity computation between all query-documenttoken pairs (O(LqLd)O(L\_q L\_d)O(Lq​Ld​)). This is more expensive than a dot product but vastly cheaper than full [cross-attention](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/cross-attention-encoder-decoder-transformers) (O((Lq+Ld)2)O((L\_q + L\_d)^2)O((Lq​+Ld​)2) per layer). Because documents can be pre-encoded and indexed, ColBERT achieves latency much closer to bi-encoders while retaining much of the cross-encoder's ability to model fine-grained interactions. Recent improvements in ColBERTv2 introduce residual compression and denoised supervision, further reducing storage requirements and improving retrieval accuracy. These models represent a promising middle ground for applications where full cross-encoders are too slow but [bi-encoders](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) are insufficiently accurate.

### [Link to Approximate Reranking](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#approximate-reranking) Approximate RerankingLink Copied

For extremely large candidate sets, you can apply vector quantization techniques from [Product Quantization](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/product-quantization.qmd) to cross-encoder representations, or use knowledge distillation to train a smaller student model to mimic the large cross-encoder's scores. Distillation involves training a compact student model (perhaps a smaller [transformer](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/transformer-attention-is-all-you-need) or even a [bi-encoder](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders)) to reproduce the ranking decisions of the large cross-encoder teacher. The student learns to approximate the teacher's score function, achieving much faster inference while preserving much of the accuracy. This approach is particularly effective when combined with the cascade architecture: the student model filters the bulk of candidates, and the teacher model verifies only the most promising ones.

## [Link to Worked Example](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#worked-example) Worked ExampleLink Copied

Let us walk through a concrete reranking scenario. Suppose you have built a technical support system for a software company. You ask:

> "How do I reset my password if I forgot the email associated with my account?"

Your [bi-encoder](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) retrieval system returns these three candidates:

**Doc A**: "To reset your password, click 'Forgot Password' on the login page. Enter your registered email address, and we will send you a reset link."

**Doc B**: "Account recovery options include password reset via email, security questions, or contacting support. If you no longer have access to your registered email, use the alternative verification methods available in your account settings."

**Doc C**: "Email notifications can be configured in your account settings. You can choose to receive updates about password changes, login attempts, and security alerts to any verified email address."

The bi-encoder ranks these based on embedding similarity: Doc A (score: 0.89), Doc C (score: 0.85), Doc B (score: 0.82). Doc A mentions "reset your password" and "email" prominently, so it has high lexical overlap. Doc C mentions "email" and "password" but is about notifications, not recovery.

Now you apply a cross-encoder. The model processes the full concatenated texts:

- For Doc A, it recognizes that the query asks about forgotten email, but the document assumes you know your email ("Enter your registered email address"). The \[ [CLS](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/special-tokens-transformers-cls-sep-pad-mask)\] representation captures this mismatch. The [cross-attention](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/cross-attention-encoder-decoder-transformers) mechanism highlights the tension between "forgot the email" in the query and "Enter your registered email" in the document, recognizing that the document presupposes knowledge that the user lacks.

- For Doc B, it identifies the phrase "no longer have access to your registered email" as directly addressing the query's condition. The cross-attention highlights the alignment between "forgot the email" in the query and "no longer have access to your registered email" in the document. It also recognizes "alternative verification methods" as the solution to the specific constraint posed by the query.

- For Doc C, it recognizes that while "email" and "password" appear, they are in the context of notifications, not recovery. The [attention weights](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/attention-mechanism-intuition-soft-lookup-weights-context-vectors) between "forgot" and the document tokens remain weak, indicating a lack of substantive connection to the query's core intent.


The cross-encoder scores might be: Doc B (0.94), Doc A (0.31), Doc C (0.12). The reranker correctly identifies that Doc B answers the specific question, while Doc A, despite high lexical similarity, fails to address the constraint about forgotten email addresses. This example illustrates how cross-encoders capture pragmatic constraints and presuppositions that [bi-encoders](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) miss.

We begin by loading a [bi-encoder](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) for initial retrieval. We'll use a small model suitable for demonstration, though production systems might use larger [embeddings](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/long-term-knowledge-storage-and-retrieval). The bi-encoder encodes both the query and documents into dense vectors that capture semantic meaning, allowing us to quickly identify the subset of documents that are most likely to be relevant based on [vector similarity](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/vector-similarity-search-metrics-ann-faiss).

In\[9\]:

Code

```
## Load bi-encoder for initial retrieval
print("Loading bi-encoder...")
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import numpy as np

bi_encoder = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

## Encode corpus (this would be precomputed and indexed in production)
print("Encoding documents...")
doc_embeddings = bi_encoder.encode(documents, convert_to_tensor=True)
query_embedding = bi_encoder.encode([query], convert_to_tensor=True)

## Compute similarities
similarities = cosine_similarity(
    query_embedding.cpu().numpy(), doc_embeddings.cpu().numpy()
)[0]

## Get top-k from bi-encoder
k = 4  # Retrieve top 4 for reranking
top_k_indices = np.argsort(similarities)[::-1][:k]
top_k_docs = [documents[i] for i in top_k_indices]
top_k_scores = similarities[top_k_indices]
```

2. [Score: 0.698] Account recovery options include password reset via email, security questions, o...
3. [Score: 0.473] If you suspect unauthorized access to your account, immediately change your pass...
4. [Score: 0.447] Email notifications can be configured in your account settings. You can choose t...
```

The [bi-encoder](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) returns candidates based on semantic similarity. Notice that documents mentioning "password" and "email" rank highly, but the specific condition about forgetting the email is not well captured by [vector similarity](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/vector-similarity-search-metrics-ann-faiss) alone. The bi-encoder identifies documents in the correct semantic neighborhood but lacks the discriminative power to verify whether the specific constraint mentioned in the query is satisfied by each document.

Now we load a cross-encoder to rerank these candidates. Cross-encoders are typically larger models that process query-document pairs jointly. Unlike the bi-encoder, which produced independent vectors, the cross-encoder will process the concatenated text of the query with each candidate document, allowing fine-grained interaction between the query's specific constraints and the document's content.

In\[11\]:

Code

## Load cross-encoder for reranking
print("Loading cross-encoder...")
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder
import time

cross_encoder = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")

## Prepare pairs for cross-encoder
pairs = [[query, doc] for doc in top_k_docs]

## Time the reranking
start_time = time.time()
cross_scores = cross_encoder.predict(pairs)
rerank_time = time.time() - start_time

## Sort by cross-encoder scores (argsort gives indices of sorted elements, so reranked_indices[i] is the original position)
reranked_order = np.argsort(cross_scores)[::-1]
reranked_docs = [top_k_docs[i] for i in reranked_order]
reranked_scores = cross_scores[reranked_order]
## For display: reranked_indices[i] should give the original rank (1-indexed) of the i-th reranked doc
reranked_indices = reranked_order + 1  # Convert to 1-indexed original ranks
```

The document about alternative verification methods for lost email access should now rank higher than the generic password reset instructions. This reordering demonstrates how the cross-encoder identifies the specific relevance signals that distinguish a satisfactory answer from a superficially similar but ultimately unhelpful document.

Out\[16\]:

Console

```
Query: How do I reset my password if I forgot the email associated with my account?

Top ranked document: To reset your password, click 'Forgot Password' on the login page. Enter your registered email addre...
```

The attention visualization reveals how the cross-encoder aligns specific query tokens with relevant document tokens. Unlike the [bi-encoder](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders), which produces a single similarity score, the cross-encoder can match "forgot" with "no longer have access" even though these phrases share no words, demonstrating the power of contextualized [cross-attention](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/cross-attention-encoder-decoder-transformers). This ability to recognize semantic equivalence across different phrasings is what enables the cross-encoder to correctly identify Doc B as the most relevant, despite its lower lexical overlap with the query compared to Doc A.

## [Link to Key Parameters](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#key-parameters) Key ParametersLink Copied

The key parameters for the retrieve-then-rerank implementation are:

- k: Number of candidates to retrieve for reranking (reranking budget). Higher values improve recall for the reranker but increase latency linearly. Selecting the appropriate value for kkk requires balancing the probability that the relevant document appears in the top kkk (recall) against the latency constraints of your application. In practice, kkk is often determined empirically by measuring the recall@k of the initial retriever on a validation set and choosing the smallest kkk that achieves acceptable recall.

- convert\_to\_tensor: Whether to return PyTorch tensors for the [embeddings](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/long-term-knowledge-storage-and-retrieval), enabling GPU acceleration for similarity computations. Using GPU acceleration for both [bi-encoder](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) and cross-encoder operations is essential for achieving acceptable latency in production systems. The tensor format also facilitates batch processing, which can significantly improve throughput.

- model: The specific cross-encoder architecture (e.g., `cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2`). Larger models provide better accuracy at the cost of increased inference time. Model selection involves trading off between the improved discrimination of larger architectures (such as large [BERT](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/bert-bidirectional-pretraining-revolutionizes-language-understanding) or [RoBERTa](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/roberta-robustly-optimized-bert-pretraining) variants) and the latency requirements of your use case. Models trained on MS MARCO, a large-scale passage ranking dataset, generally provide strong zero-shot performance for general-domain retrieval tasks.

- max\_length: Maximum sequence length for the cross-encoder (typically 512 tokens). Documents longer than this are truncated, potentially losing relevant information at the end. When working with long documents, consider using the sliding window approach discussed earlier, or select models specifically designed for long sequences (such as those using [Longformer](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/attention-complexity-quadratic-scaling-memory-efficient-transformers) or [BigBird](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/bigbird-sparse-attention-random-connections-long-documents) architectures) that can handle thousands of tokens.

## [Link to Limitations and Impact](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#limitations-and-impact) Limitations and ImpactLink Copied

While cross-encoderreranking dramatically improves retrieval accuracy, it introduces significant operational constraints that shape system architecture. The [quadratic complexity](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/attention-complexity-quadratic-scaling-memory-efficient-transformers) with respect to sequence length means that processing long documents becomes prohibitively expensive. You cannot easily rerank entire books or lengthy research papers without aggressive truncation or chunking, which risks losing relevant information buried in the middle or end of texts. This limitation has driven research into efficient transformers and hierarchical approaches that can process long documents without quadratic scaling.

Furthermore, the lack of precomputation requires running a forward pass through a large [transformer](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/transformer-attention-is-all-you-need) for every query-document pair at query time, creating a throughput bottleneck that limits the number of candidates you can rerank within latency budgets. Unlike [bi-encoders](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders), which amortize document encoding over all queries, cross-encoders must reprocess documents for every new query. This architectural constraint means that cross-encoders scale with query volume in ways that can strain GPU resources during traffic spikes.

These limitations have spurred the development of intermediate architectures like [ColBERT](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/multi-vector-retrievers-fine-grained-token-level-matching-for-neural-information-retrieval) and SPLADE that attempt to preserve token-level interaction capabilities while maintaining indexable representations. They also motivate the cascade approaches mentioned earlier, where you progressively filter candidates through increasingly expensive models. The impact of reranking on the field has been profound: it established the two-stage retrieve-then-rerank paradigm as the standard for [neural information retrieval](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/neural-information-retrieval-semantic-search), allowing systems to balance the competing demands of recall, precision, and latency. Without reranking, dense retrieval systems would struggle to achieve the accuracy required for production search engines and retrieval-augmented generation applications, where returning an irrelevant document in the top position significantly degrades user experience. As we move toward more sophisticated [RAG](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-passage-retrieval-retrieval-augmented-generation-rag) pipelines in upcoming chapters on [RAG Prompt Engineering](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/rag-prompt-engineering-context-citations) and Evaluation, the reranker serves as a critical quality filter, ensuring that only the most relevant context reaches the language generator.

Advertisement

## [Link to Summary](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/reranking-cross-encoders-information-retrieval\#summary) SummaryLink Copied

Reranking addresses the fundamental limitation of [bi-encoder](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/dense-retrieval-semantic-search-bi-encoders) retrieval systems: the inability to model fine-grained interactions between queries and documents due to independent encoding. By processing query-document pairs through [cross-attention](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/cross-attention-encoder-decoder-transformers) mechanisms, cross-encoders capture nuanced relevance signals that [vector similarity](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/vector-similarity-search-metrics-ann-faiss) misses.

The standard retrieve-then-rerank pipeline uses fast bi-encoders to recall a candidate set, then applies computationally intensive cross-encoders to precisely rank the top candidates. Training these models involves [binary classification](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/logistic-regression-complete-guide-mathematical-foundations-python-implementation), [pairwise ranking](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/bradley-terry-model-pairwise-preferences-rankings) losses, or listwise objectives, with [hard negative mining](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/contrastive-learning-retrieval-infonce-dpr) being essential for learning discriminative representations. The curriculum of hard negatives, progressing from easy to difficult examples, enables the model to learn the subtle distinctions necessary to improve upon initial retrieval.

The primary constraint is latency: cross-encoders are orders of magnitude slower than bi-encoders due to their inability to precompute document representations. This necessitates careful budgeting of the number of candidates to rerank, cascade architectures with multiple ranking stages, and the exploration of efficient alternatives like late interaction models.

When implementing retrieval systems, you should view reranking not as an optional enhancement but as a necessary component for achieving high precision, particularly in domains where subtle distinctions determine relevance. The choice of reranking budget involves trading accuracy for latency, a decision that depends on your specific application requirements and user expectations. As retrieval-augmented generation systems become increasingly central to AI applications, the reranker's role in ensuring high-quality context selection becomes ever more critical to overall system performance.

# [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 ""): Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking

Report issue for preceding element

Feng Wang1  Yuqing Li1,2  Han Xiao1

1Jina AI GmbH  2University of Pittsburgh

Prinzessinnenstraße 19, 10969, Berlin, Germany

research@jina.ai

Report issue for preceding element

###### Abstract

Report issue for preceding element

[jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") is a 0.6B parameter multilingual document reranker that introduces a novel _last but not late_ interaction. Unlike late interaction models such as ColBERT that perform separate encoding followed by multi-vector matching, our approach conducts causal self-attention between query and documents within the same context window, enabling rich cross-document interactions before extracting contextual embeddings from the last token of each document. This compact architecture achieves state-of-the-art BEIR performance with 61.94 nDCG@10 while being 10×10\\times smaller than generative listwise rerankers.

Report issue for preceding element

## 1 Introduction

Report issue for preceding element

Neural document reranking faces a fundamental efficiency-effectiveness tradeoff. Cross-encoders achieve strong performance through joint query-document processing but require separate forward passes for each pair, while embedding models enable efficient similarity computation but lose fine-grained interaction signals. Recent innovations have attempted to bridge this gap through different interaction approaches. Late interaction models like ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib13 "")) and their variants (Liu et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib15 ""); Chaffin and Sourty, [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib7 "")) separately encode queries and documents into multi-vector representations, then perform interaction through token-level similarity operations.

Report issue for preceding element

We introduce [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 ""), which proposes a novel _last but not late_ interaction (LBNL) that fundamentally differs from existing approaches. While late interaction models delay attention interactions until after separate encoding, our method performs causal query-document self-attention within the transformer context window first, enabling documents to attend to each other and establish cross-document relationships before extracting contextual embeddings from the _last_ token of each document. This cross-document interaction capability is unavailable in separate encoding approaches and represents the core innovation of our method. Unlike late interaction models that perform interactions after encoding, we enable interactions during encoding, making our approach _not late_.

Report issue for preceding element

Our evaluation shows significant improvements across diverse benchmarks. [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") achieves 61.94 nDCG@10 on BEIR, representing the highest score among all evaluated rerankers and a 4.88% improvement over our previous jina-reranker-v2. The model excels particularly in multi-hop retrieval with HotpotQA reaching 78.56, fact verification achieving 93.95 on FEVER, competitive multilingual performance across 18 languages at 66.50 on MIRACL, and specialized domain coverage reaching 63.28 on code retrieval.

Report issue for preceding element

## 2 Related Work

Report issue for preceding element

Document reranking approaches can be categorized by their interactions and learning objectives. Traditional learning-to-rank methods (Bruch et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib3 "")) include pointwise approaches that predict relevance scores independently, pairwise methods like RankNet (Burges et al., [2005](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib4 "")) that compare document pairs, and listwise techniques that optimize global ranking objectives. Cross-encoders like BERT-based rerankers (Nogueira and Cho, [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib17 "")) achieve strong performance through full query-document interaction but require separate forward passes for each pair, creating computational bottlenecks for large-scale retrieval. Recent comparative studies (Déjean et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib9 "")) demonstrate that while LLM-based rerankers show impressive zero-shot capabilities, traditional cross-encoders remain highly competitive across diverse retrieval scenarios.

Report issue for preceding element

Late interaction models represent a significant approach that balances efficiency with expressiveness. ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib13 "")) exemplifies this approach by independently encoding queries and documents into multi-vector representations, then computing similarity through MaxSim operations over token-level embeddings. This design enables pre-computation of document representations while preserving fine-grained matching signals. Recent developments have expanded this approach: analysis of matching mechanisms and token pruning strategies (Liu et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib15 "")) provides theoretical foundations, LITE (Ji et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib12 "")) introduces learnable late interactions, and Jina-ColBERT-v2 (Zhang et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib30 "")) extends the approach to multilingual settings. PyLate (Chaffin and Sourty, [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib7 "")) provides flexible frameworks for training and deployment of such models. The late chunking method (Günther et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib10 "")) processes complete documents through transformers before applying chunking boundaries, extracting chunk-level embeddings that preserve contextual relationships. This approach demonstrates how leveraging broader document context can improve embedding quality, though it focuses primarily on retrieval rather than reranking applications.

Report issue for preceding element

LLM-powered reranker has emerged as a powerful family with diverse implementations. These approaches can be categorized into discriminative and generative methods. Generative approaches like RankGPT (Qin et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib19 "")) prompt LLMs to generate ranked lists, leveraging their reasoning capabilities for relevance assessment, but typically require large models for competitive performance. Fine-tuning methods like RankVicuna (Pradeep et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib18 "")) adapt existing models for relevance scoring tasks. Efficiency-focused innovations include FIRST (Reddy et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib21 "")), which accelerates inference through single-token decoding, and PE-Rank (Qin et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib20 "")), which leverages passage embeddings to reduce computational latency by 4.5×4.5\\times. Recent advances in training methodology include ERank (Cai et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib5 "")), which combines supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning for improved ranking quality, and the Qwen3 Embedding series (Zhang et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib31 "")), which demonstrates sophisticated multi-stage training pipelines. DeAR (Abdallah et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib1 "")) introduces dual-stage reasoning with LLM distillation for enhanced cross-document analysis.

Report issue for preceding element

## 3 Model Architecture

Report issue for preceding element

### 3.1 Architecture

Report issue for preceding element![Refer to caption](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2/x1.png)Figure 1: Architecture of [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") showing the transformer backbone with special token positions for embedding extraction. The model processes multiple documents and query in one context window, extracting contextual embeddings at designated token positions for similarity computation.Report issue for preceding element

[jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") implements an interaction architecture that fundamentally differs from existing approaches. Built upon Qwen3-0.6B (Yang et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib28 "")) with 28 transformer layers, 1024 hidden dimensions, 16 attention heads, and 131K token context capacity, our approach processes queries and multiple documents simultaneously within shared context windows. We add a lightweight MLP projector (1024→\\rightarrow512→\\rightarrow256 dimensions) to transform contextual representations into ranking-optimized embeddings. Table [5](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#A1.T5 "Table 5 ‣ Appendix A Model Configuration and Training Details ‣ jina-reranker-v3: Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking") provides complete architectural specifications.

Report issue for preceding element

Our architecture addresses fundamental limitations in existing interactions. ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib13 "")) achieves efficiency through separate encoding followed by multi-vector interaction, but cannot capture early query-document interactions during encoding or enable cross-document interactions within the attention mechanism. Our approach enables causal self-attention interaction within the transformer architecture, allowing documents to attend to each other and the query simultaneously, then extracts contextually-informed embeddings from designated token positions.

Report issue for preceding element

The core innovation lies in enabling cross-document interactions during encoding: instead of delaying interaction until after separate encoding as in late interaction models, we process all documents and the query simultaneously within shared context windows. This allows each document to attend to other documents and observe their content, enabling contextual embeddings that capture not just query-document relevance but also inter-document relationships and comparative context. Such cross-document interactions are impossible in separate encoding approaches and represent a fundamental advancement in reranking architecture.

Report issue for preceding element

We extract contextual embeddings at designated special token positions: 𝐪~=𝐇tq\\tilde{\\mathbf{q}}=\\mathbf{H}\_{t\_{q}} and 𝐝~i=𝐇ti\\tilde{\\mathbf{d}}\_{i}=\\mathbf{H}\_{t\_{i}} where tqt\_{q} and tit\_{i} are positions of the special tokens and 𝐇\\mathbf{H} represents the transformer’s final layer hidden states after causal self-attention. These embeddings capture both local document semantics and global cross-document context through the shared attention mechanism, enabling rich inter-document interactions unavailable in separate encoding approaches.

Report issue for preceding element

A two-layer projection network with ReLU activation maps the 1024-dimensional hidden states to 256-dimensional embedding space: 𝐪=Pϕ​(𝐪~)\\mathbf{q}=P\_{\\phi}(\\tilde{\\mathbf{q}}) and 𝐝i=Pϕ​(𝐝~i)\\mathbf{d}\_{i}=P\_{\\phi}(\\tilde{\\mathbf{d}}\_{i}). Relevance scores are computed via cosine similarity: si=cos⁡(𝐪,𝐝i)s\_{i}=\\cos(\\mathbf{q},\\mathbf{d}\_{i}). This architecture combines the expressiveness of joint encoding with efficient similarity computation.

Report issue for preceding element

For document collections exceeding the 131K token context limit, we process documents in batches of up to 64 documents per forward pass, with query embeddings maintained consistently across batches to ensure ranking coherence.

Report issue for preceding element

### 3.2 Prompt Template

Report issue for preceding element

[jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") processes structured prompts following Qwen3’s instruction format with system/user/assistant roles to leverage existing instruction-following capabilities. As shown in Table [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#S3.T1 "Table 1 ‣ 3.2 Prompt Template ‣ 3 Model Architecture ‣ jina-reranker-v3: Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking"), the system prompt establishes a search relevance expert persona, while the user prompt provides clear ranking instructions with dual query placement.

Report issue for preceding element

| Prompt Template |
| --- |
| <\|im\_start\|>system |
| You are a search relevance expert who can determine |
| a ranking of passages based on their relevance to the query. |
| <\|im\_end\|> |

| I will provide you with k passages, each indicated by a numerical identifier. |
| Rank the passages based on their relevance to query: \[QUERY\] |
| <passage id="0"> |
| \[DOCUMENT\_1\]<\|doc\_emb\|> |
| </passage> |

| </passage> |
| ... |

| \[DOCUMENT\_k\]<\|doc\_emb\|> |
| </passage> |
| <query> |
| \[QUERY\]<\|query\_emb\|> |

| <\|im\_end\|> |

Table 1: Complete prompt template structure used by [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 ""). Special tokens <\|doc\_emb\|> and <\|query\_emb\|> mark positions for embedding extraction from transformer hidden states. The empty <think></think> tag serves as a placeholder without reasoning content.Report issue for preceding element

The template strategically places the query both at the beginning for instructions and at the end for final attention, sandwiching all documents in between. This design enables the final query position to attend to all preceding documents through causal attention while maintaining clear task instructions. Special tokens <\|doc\_emb\|> after each document and <\|query\_emb\|> after the final query mark specific positions for embedding extraction from transformer hidden states. While Qwen3 supports thinking capabilities, our implementation uses an empty <think></think> placeholder since the model is not trained with reasoning traces for document reranking tasks.

## 4 Training

Report issue for preceding element

[jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") employs a comprehensive multi-objective training approach combining InfoNCE loss with specialized auxiliary losses to optimize ranking performance across diverse domains.

Report issue for preceding element

The core training objective integrates multiple loss components, each addressing distinct aspects of the ranking problem:

Report issue for preceding element

|     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | ℓ=ℓrank+0.45⋅ℓdisperse+0.85⋅ℓdual+0.85⋅ℓsimilar\\ell=\\ell\_{\\mathrm{rank}}+0.45\\cdot\\ell\_{\\mathrm{disperse}}+0.85\\cdot\\ell\_{\\mathrm{dual}}+0.85\\cdot\\ell\_{\\mathrm{similar}} |  | (1) |

The primary component is the InfoNCE loss ℓrank\\ell\_{\\mathrm{rank}}(van den Oord et al., [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib24 "")), which generates the core ranking signal through contrastive learning with hard negatives:

Report issue for preceding element

|     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | ℓrank=−1N​∑i=1Nlog⁡es​(𝐪i,𝐝i+)/τZiwhereZi=es​(𝐪i,𝐝i+)/τ+∑k=1Kes​(𝐪i,𝐝i,k−)/τ\\ell\_{\\mathrm{rank}}=-\\frac{1}{N}\\sum\_{i=1}^{N}\\log\\frac{e^{s(\\mathbf{q}\_{i},\\mathbf{d}\_{i}^{+})/\\tau}}{Z\_{i}}\\quad\\text{where}\\quad Z\_{i}=e^{s(\\mathbf{q}\_{i},\\mathbf{d}\_{i}^{+})/\\tau}+\\sum\_{k=1}^{K}e^{s(\\mathbf{q}\_{i},\\mathbf{d}\_{i,k}^{-})/\\tau} |  | (2) |

Here, 𝐪i\\mathbf{q}\_{i} denotes the query embedding, 𝐝i+\\mathbf{d}\_{i}^{+} represents the positive document embedding, 𝐝i,k−\\mathbf{d}\_{i,k}^{-} denotes one of KK negative document embeddings, s​(⋅,⋅)s(\\cdot,\\cdot) is the cosine similarity function, τ\\tau is the temperature parameter, and NN is the batch size.

Report issue for preceding element

To prevent representation collapse, we incorporate the dispersive loss ℓdisperse\\ell\_{\\mathrm{disperse}}(Wang et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib25 "")), which enhances embedding diversity by maximizing the average pairwise cosine distance between document embeddings:

Report issue for preceding element

|     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | ℓdisperse=1N​log⁡1K+1​(es​(𝐝i+,𝐝i,k−)/τ+∑k=1K−1es​(𝐝i,k−,𝐝i,k+1−)/τ)\\ell\_{\\mathrm{disperse}}=\\frac{1}{N}\\log\\frac{1}{K+1}\\left(e^{s(\\mathbf{d}\_{i}^{+},\\mathbf{d}\_{i,k}^{-})/\\tau}+\\sum\_{k=1}^{K-1}e^{s(\\mathbf{d}\_{i,k}^{-},\\mathbf{d}\_{i,k+1}^{-})/\\tau}\\right) |  | (3) |

The dual matching loss ℓdual\\ell\_{\\mathrm{dual}}111During training, the special token <\|query\_emb\|> is inserted at the end of the query at the beginning of the input sequence. follows the same formulation as Eq. [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#S4.E2 "In 4 Training ‣ jina-reranker-v3: Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking") but computes the query embedding from the query tokens at the sequence start. This enforces bidirectional consistency between query-to-document and document-to-query similarity scores, enhancing ranking robustness.

Report issue for preceding element

Finally, the similarity loss ℓsimilar\\ell\_{\\mathrm{similar}}(Huang et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib11 "")) maintains semantic coherence at the document level. For each document in the input set, we create an augmented duplicate 𝐝i∗\\mathbf{d}\_{i}^{\*} through text augmentation techniques. The loss then treats the original document and its augmented version as a positive pair, while other documents serve as negatives. This encourages consistent embedding representations for semantically equivalent documents, even when their surface forms differ due to augmentation.

Report issue for preceding element

The training methodology follows a progressive three-stage approach designed for systematic complexity scaling:

Report issue for preceding element

Stage 1: Foundation Specialization. Starting from pretrained Qwen3-0.6B, we simultaneously train domain-specific configurations using LoRA fine-tuning with rr=16 and α\\alpha=32 targeting all attention and FFN layers while freezing the backbone. The model processes training sequences containing 16 documents per query (one positive and 15 negative examples), with each document truncated or padded to 768 tokens, yielding a maximal total sequence length of 12,288 tokens. Training data is drawn from diverse datasets including BGE-M3 (Chen et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib8 "")) for multilingual coverage across 15 languages, Cornstack (Suresh et al., [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib22 "")) for code retrieval, as well as specialized datasets for biomedical (Xu et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib27 "")) and instruction following (Weller et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib26 "")) configurations.

Report issue for preceding element

Stage 2: Context and Hard Negative Mining. This stage combines context scaling and comprehensive robustness optimization. Context scaling extends sequence lengths to 8,192 tokens through configurations like mldr-8192 for long documents using multilingual MLDR datasets, jina-crosslingual for enhanced multilingual capabilities, and biomed for medical domains. Simultaneously, cross-system hard negative mining ensures robustness through specialized optimizations including jina-en-v2 for English performance, miracl-v2 for multilingual retrieval, cornstack-v2 for code understanding, and context-chunk-v3 for long-document processing. Training systematically mines hard negatives across multiple retrieval systems including BGE, Jina, GTE, and E5-Large with up to 25 negatives per query and very low temperature of 0.05, using key datasets including MS-MARCO Campos et al. ( [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib6 "")), mMARCO Bonifacio et al. ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib2 "")), and domain-specific synthetic question-answer pairs.

Report issue for preceding element

Stage 3: Model Ensemble and Optimization. The final stage combines multiple specialized models trained in previous stages through linear model merging. Each domain-specific model contributes weighted expertise, with merge weights ranging from 0.25 to 0.65 based on domain importance and performance. This approach enables the final model to leverage diverse domain knowledge while maintaining architectural efficiency.

Report issue for preceding element

Detailed hyperparameter evolution across stages demonstrates multi-objective optimization with stage-tailored configurations (see Appendix [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#A1.T6 "Table 6 ‣ Appendix A Model Configuration and Training Details ‣ jina-reranker-v3: Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking")). Foundation stages use aggressive learning rates of 5e-5 with substantial negative sampling of 15 negatives. Context scaling stages reduce batch sizes dramatically from 60 to 6 to accommodate 8K sequences while employing conservative learning rates of 6e-6. Loss weight adaptation varies across different domain specializations, with dispersive loss typically set to 0.45, dual-matching loss ranging from 0.65 to 0.85, and similarity loss stabilizing around 0.75 to 0.85 depending on the specific domain requirements.

## 5 Evaluation

Report issue for preceding element

We evaluate [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") across diverse multilingual retrieval benchmarks to demonstrate its architectural innovations and training efficiency. Our comprehensive assessment reveals how the dual embedding architecture with LBNL interaction enables superior performance density, achieving state-of-the-art results with remarkable parameter efficiency.

Report issue for preceding element

### 5.1 Experimental Setup

Report issue for preceding element

Our evaluation spans four challenging benchmarks that test different aspects of ranking capability. BEIR Thakur et al. ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib23 "")) represents the gold standard for English retrieval evaluation, encompassing 13 heterogeneous tasks from question answering on Natural Questions to fact verification on FEVER, testing the model’s ability to generalize across domains without task-specific optimization. MIRACL Zhang et al. ( [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib29 "")) pushes multilingual boundaries with 18 languages spanning diverse linguistic families, from Arabic and Chinese to Finnish and Thai, requiring deep cross-lingual understanding. MKQA Longpre et al. ( [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib16 "")) specifically challenges cross-lingual question answering capabilities, while CoIR Li et al. ( [2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#bib.bib14 "")) focuses on the specialized domain of code retrieval, where semantic understanding of programming constructs becomes crucial.

Report issue for preceding element

We establish rigorous baselines spanning both retrieval approaches. First-stage dense retrievers include jina-embeddings-v3, providing the foundation top-100 candidates that all rerankers process. Second-stage rerankers encompass our previous jina-reranker-v2, the multilingual bge-reranker-v2-m3, the mxbai-rerank variants at different scales, and Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B and Qwen3-Reranker-4B models. This comprehensive comparison enables analysis across both architectural approaches and parameter scaling effects.

Report issue for preceding element

All evaluations follow identical protocols using nDCG@10 as the primary metric, with our own controlled runs ensuring fair comparison. The consistent use of jina-embeddings-v3 retrieval candidates eliminates retrieval variance and isolates reranking effectiveness.

Report issue for preceding element

### 5.2 Overall Performance Across Benchmarks

Report issue for preceding element

Table [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#S5.T2 "Table 2 ‣ 5.2 Overall Performance Across Benchmarks ‣ 5 Evaluation ‣ jina-reranker-v3: Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking") demonstrates [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "")’s exceptional performance density across diverse evaluation scenarios. On BEIR, our model achieves the highest score among all rerankers at 61.94, establishing new state-of-the-art performance for English retrieval. This represents a 4.88% improvement over our previous jina-reranker-v2 at 57.06, directly attributable to LBNL interaction mechanism where query and document embeddings are extracted from shared forward passes rather than separate encoding pipelines.

Report issue for preceding element

Parameter efficiency analysis reveals striking advantages compared to larger alternatives. Against the 1.5B parameter mxbai-rerank-large-v2, [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") achieves superior BEIR performance with 61.94 versus 61.44 using 2.5×2.5\\times fewer parameters, while providing specialized domain coverage unavailable in competing models reaching 63.28 on CoIR. This efficiency derives from architectural innovations: Qwen3’s optimized transformer backbone combined with our specialized 256-dimensional projector network that concentrates ranking signals without requiring massive parameter scaling.

Report issue for preceding element

Multilingual evaluation reveals strong cross-lingual capabilities despite the model’s compact architecture. The 66.50 score on MIRACL, while 2.82 points below the multilingual-specialized bge-reranker-v2-m3 at 69.32, demonstrates effective knowledge transfer from our progressive training methodology. The 67.84 MKQA performance closely approaches jina-reranker-v2’s 67.90, indicating that architectural sophistication can partially offset parameter differences in multilingual scenarios.

### 5.3 English Retrieval Performance on BEIR

Report issue for preceding element

Table [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.25085v2#S5.T3 "Table 3 ‣ 5.3 English Retrieval Performance on BEIR ‣ 5 Evaluation ‣ jina-reranker-v3: Last but Not Late Interaction for Document Reranking") provides granular analysis across BEIR’s heterogeneous tasks, revealing specific architectural advantages. The model achieves consistent excellence across diverse reasoning tasks, with particularly strong performance on complex multi-hop reasoning reaching 78.56 on HotpotQA and fact verification achieving 93.95 on FEVER. These results highlight how LBNL interaction enables sophisticated query-document self-attention during encoding, capturing evidence relationships that separate encoding approaches miss.

Report issue for preceding element

Within the same scale category, [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") reveals significant advantages. Against bge-reranker-v2-m3 with the same 0.6B parameters, [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") delivers a substantial 5.43% improvement from 56.51 to 61.94, demonstrating architectural innovation over simple parameter scaling. The specialized 256-dimensional projector network effectively concentrates ranking signals while preserving contextual representations from the Qwen3 backbone. Remarkably, our model surpasses mxbai-rerank-large-v2’s 61.44 performance while using 2.5×2.5\\times fewer parameters, establishing that sophisticated architecture can surpass brute-force scaling approaches.

Since [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") processes all documents simultaneously in a listwise manner within shared context windows, we investigate the sensitivity to document ordering. We evaluate three variants: documents ordered by descending relevance scores (D), ascending scores (A), and random permutation (R). The results show modest variations across orderings, with random ordering (R) achieving the highest average of 62.54, followed by descending (D) at 61.94 and ascending (A) at 61.52. While the differences are not conclusive, this analysis reveals that the LBNL interaction maintains relatively stable performance across different input orderings, suggesting robust self-attention mechanisms that can effectively process documents regardless of their initial arrangement.

Report issue for preceding element

The model’s dominance extends particularly to question-answering scenarios, where Natural Questions achieves 74.30 and argumentative retrieval on ArguAna reaches 77.39, showcasing the benefit of contextual embeddings. These tasks require understanding complex query intent and matching it against nuanced document semantics, precisely the scenario where our dual embedding extraction approach provides maximum advantage over traditional cross-encoder scoring.

## 6 Conclusion

Report issue for preceding element

We present [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 ""), a 0.6B parameter multilingual document reranker that introduces _last but not late_ interaction for efficient document reranking. Our approach enables cross-document interactions during encoding by processing queries and multiple documents simultaneously within shared context windows, then extracting contextual embeddings from designated special token positions.

Report issue for preceding element

By effectively adapting modern long-context language models for document reranking, [jina-reranker-v3](https://huggingface.co/jinaai/jina-reranker-v3 "") demonstrates that discriminative methods can bridge the efficiency-effectiveness gap while maintaining significant computational advantages over generative alternatives. The results show strong performance across diverse benchmarks, with particular excellence in complex retrieval tasks and multilingual scenarios.
